pages.title: Assertion
pages.summary: Assertion is a debugging utility supported on compute capability 2.x and higher that halts kernel execution if an expression evaluates to zero, triggering a debugger breakpoint or printing a formatted failure message to stderr.

pages.title: Breakpoint Function
pages.summary: The __brkpt() function suspends the execution of a kernel function when invoked from any device thread.

pages.title: 64-Bit Compatibility
pages.summary: The 64-bit version of the nvcc compiler compiles device code in 64-bit mode, requiring that the host code be compiled in 64-bit mode for support.

pages.title: Controlling Access Rights
pages.summary: Explains how Virtual Memory Management APIs enable explicit device-level access control for mapped virtual address ranges using cuMemSetAccess, contrasting this granular approach with the automatic mapping of cudaEnablePeerAccess.

pages.title: Address Operator
pages.summary: The address-of operator (`&`) is prohibited when applied to built-in variables in CUDA C++.

pages.title: 10.16 Address Space Conversion Functions (Overview)
pages.summary: Overview of CUDA C++ address space conversion functions. Enables converting pointers from/to other representations for PTX interoperability and performance optimizations (e.g., 32-bit truncation for shared/local/const spaces). Roundtrip guarantees equivalence.

pages.title: ADL Lookup Issues
pages.summary: Extended __device__ lambdas passed to host code are replaced by placeholder types containing the enclosing function's address, causing unintended namespaces to participate in ADL and potentially leading to ambiguity.

pages.title: Application Compatibility
pages.summary: Application compatibility enables execution across varying GPU architectures by embedding binary and PTX code via compiler options, facilitating automatic runtime selection or just-in-time compilation, and providing macros for conditional code paths.

pages.title: Assertion
pages.summary: Assertion is a debugging utility supported on compute capability 2.x and higher that halts kernel execution if an expression evaluates to zero, triggering a debugger breakpoint or printing a formatted failure message to stderr.

pages.title: Assignment Operator
pages.summary: CUDA assignment operator restrictions prohibit device-side assignment to __constant__ variables, inline initialization of __shared__ variables, and assignment to built-in variables.

pages.title: Asynchronous Barrier (cuda::barrier / std::barrier)
pages.summary: GPU implementation of std::barrier for thread synchronization that supports hardware acceleration on compute capability 8.0+, temporal splitting, phase management, spatial partitioning, early exit, completion functions, and legacy C-like primitives.

pages.title: Asynchronous Concurrent Execution
pages.summary: CUDA provides mechanisms to execute host computations, device computations, and memory transfers concurrently, leveraging hardware engines and asynchronous operations to overlap data movement with kernel execution.

pages.title: Asynchronous Data Copies (memcpy_async)
pages.summary: CUDA 11+ API for overlapping computation with data movement. Enables asynchronous global-to-shared memory copies via cuda::barrier, cuda::pipeline, or cooperative_groups::wait. Includes performance guidance on alignment, trivially copyable types, and warp entanglement effects on commit/wait/arrive operations.

pages.title: Binary Compatibility
pages.summary: Binary compatibility ensures that pre-compiled CUDA binaries (cubin objects) execute correctly on newer devices within the same major compute capability revision, while platform-specific constraints limit support to desktop GPUs.

pages.title: Breakpoint Function
pages.summary: Aliases: __brkpt

pages.title: C++ Compatibility
pages.summary: CUDA source files are processed by the compiler front end using C++ syntax rules, providing full C++ support for host code while restricting device code to a subset of C++ features.

pages.title: C++ Compatibility
pages.summary: The front end of the compiler processes CUDA source files according to C++ syntax rules [CUDA_C_Programming_Guide:L1204-L1206]. Full C++ is supported for the host code [CUDA_C_Programming_Guide:L1204-L1206]. Conversely, only a subset of C++

pages.title: Call Stack
pages.summary: Call stack size can be queried and set on compute capability 2.x+ devices using cudaDeviceGetLimit() and cudaDeviceSetLimit(). Stack overflow causes kernel failure or warnings, especially for recursive functions. Manual stack size configuration is required when the compiler cannot determine it statically.

pages.title: Child Graph Memory Nodes
pages.summary: Moving child graph ownership to parent graphs enables memory allocation and free nodes within the child while imposing strict lifecycle and structural restrictions once transferred.

pages.title: Choosing the Right Page Size
pages.summary: Analyzes the trade-offs between small and large virtual page sizes regarding memory fragmentation, TLB misses, and migration latency. Highlights that GPU TLB misses are particularly costly.

pages.title: Cluster Launch Control
pages.summary: Cluster Launch Control is a Compute Capability 10.0 feature that enables asynchronous thread block cancellation to facilitate advanced work-stealing and dynamic load balancing.

pages.title: 10.18.6 Compiler Optimization Hint Restrictions
pages.summary: Restrictions for compiler optimization hint functions. __assume() only supported with cl.exe host compiler. Other functions require invocation from __device__/__global__ functions or when __CUDA_ARCH__ macro is defined, unless host compiler supports them.

pages.title: Compressible Memory
pages.summary: Compressible memory is a CUDA Virtual Memory Management (VMM) feature that accelerates data access by applying hardware compression to sparsely structured patterns, reducing DRAM and L2 cache bandwidth and capacity usage. [CUDA_C_Programming_Guide:L15101-L15128]

pages.title: Coherency and Concurrency Constraints on Pre-6.x
pages.summary: Simultaneous CPU and GPU access to managed memory is prohibited on pre-6.x architectures due to coherency limitations, requiring the GPU to have exclusive access to all managed data and mandating explicit synchronization before CPU access, even when accessing different allocations.

pages.title: Conditional Graph Nodes Overview
pages.summary: Covers conditional nodes (IF, WHILE, SWITCH) that enable dynamic control flow and looping within graphs. Evaluation happens on-device after dependencies are met. Uses condition handles created before the node, with values set via `cudaGraphSetConditional()` or defaults.

pages.title: Conditional Node Body Graph Requirements
pages.summary: Conditional node body graphs are constrained to single-device residency with specific allowed node types, restrictions on dynamic parallelism and device graph launches, and strict memory access rules for copy operations.

pages.title: Conditional SWITCH Nodes
pages.summary: SWITCH nodes, introduced in CUDA 12.8, enable dynamic branching by executing exactly one of `n` body graphs within a conditional node based on a runtime condition value, or none if the value is `>= n` [CUDA_C_Programming_Guide:L3338-L3342].

pages.title: Cooperative Groups Introduction
pages.summary: Cooperative Groups is a CUDA programming model extension introduced in CUDA 9 that organizes communicating threads into first-class objects to enable safe, flexible synchronization and richer parallel decomposition granularity.

pages.title: barrier_arrive and barrier_wait
pages.summary: barrier_arrive and barrier_wait are Cooperative Groups synchronization functions that implement a two-phase barrier mechanism using an arrival_token to allow independent work between arrival and waiting, ensuring all group threads synchronize before proceeding.

pages.title: binary_partition
pages.summary: A collective operation that partitions the parent group into two subgroups based on a boolean predicate. It is a specialized form of labeled_partition where the label is restricted to 0 or 1.

pages.title: Cooperative Groups Execution Control (invoke_one / invoke_one_broadcast)
pages.summary: Cooperative Groups execution control provides collective functions invoke_one and invoke_one_broadcast to select an arbitrary thread within a group to execute a callable, with optional return value distribution and implicit synchronization.

pages.title: Group Collectives
pages.summary: Overview of collective operations provided by the Cooperative Groups library. These operations require participation of all threads in the specified group to complete, and all threads must pass the same values for corresponding arguments unless explicitly allowed otherwise.

pages.title: inclusive_scan and exclusive_scan
pages.summary: Collective prefix sum operations that compute reductions across threads in a cooperative group, with variants supporting atomic updates.

pages.title: labeled_partition
pages.summary: A collective operation that partitions the parent group into one-dimensional subgroups based on a label value. Threads with the same label value are assigned to the same subgroup.

pages.title: memcpy_async
pages.summary: memcpy_async is a group-wide collective operation that leverages hardware-accelerated, nonblocking memory transactions to copy data from global memory to shared memory, requiring specific alignment and synchronization via wait or wait_prior calls.

pages.title: reduce
pages.summary: Performs a reduction operation on data provided by each thread in a cooperative group, supporting hardware acceleration on Compute Capability 8.0+ devices.

pages.title: Reduce Operators
pages.summary: Function objects in the cooperative_groups namespace that mirror hardware intrinsics for cg::reduce, providing hardware acceleration for integral types on CC 8.0+ while falling back to shuffle-based reductions for floats, vectors, and custom function objects.

pages.title: sync
pages.summary: Synchronizes all threads named in a cooperative group, halting execution until every thread reaches the synchronization point.

pages.title: wait and wait_prior
pages.summary: Collective synchronization primitives that block threads until asynchronous `memcpy_async` global-to-shared memory transfers complete, with `wait_prior` enabling stage-based overlapping.

pages.title: 16-Bit Floating-Point Textures
pages.summary: CUDA arrays support the IEEE 754-2008 binary2 half format, requiring device intrinsics or external libraries for conversion, automatic promotion to 32-bit floats during texture filtering, and specific channel description functions.

pages.title: CUDA Arrays
pages.summary: CUDA arrays are opaque memory layouts optimized for texture fetching, supporting 1D, 2D, and 3D dimensions with elements containing 1, 2, or 4 components of specific integer or float types, accessible only via texture or surface operations.

pages.title: Atomic Functions
pages.summary: CUDA atomic functions provide read-modify-write operations on global or shared memory, supporting device, block, and system scopes with configurable memory ordering and explicit compiler builtins introduced in CUDA 12.8+.

pages.title: CUDA C++ Execution Model
pages.summary: The CUDA C++ Execution Model extends the ISO C++ execution model.

pages.title: CUDA C++ Memory Model
pages.summary: The CUDA C++ Memory Model extends the ISO C++ Memory Model, with detailed specifications available in the dedicated CUDA C++ Memory Model documentation.

pages.title: C++17 Features (constexpr host/device rules)
pages.summary: Under relaxed constexpr rules, the CUDA compiler generates device code for __host__ constexpr functions called from device code and preserves __device__ constexpr bodies for host code, subject to execution space restrictions and potential silent runtime failures due to limited compiler diagnostics.

pages.title: CUDA C++ Code Samples
pages.summary: Example code snippets demonstrating Data Aggregation Classes, Derived Classes, Class Templates, Function Templates, and Functor Classes in CUDA.

pages.title: Compute Capabilities
pages.summary: Compute capability defines the general specifications, feature sets, compiler targets, and hardware limits of a CUDA compute device, with baseline features generally available on subsequent architectures while architecture- and family-specific features require specialized compiler suffixes.

pages.title: CUDA Compute Modes
pages.summary: CUDA compute modes, configurable via nvidia-smi, define GPU access policies including Default, Exclusive-Process, and Prohibited states, while Pascal+ architectures support instruction-level Compute Preemption to manage long-running kernels at the cost of context switch overheads.

pages.title: CUDA Context
pages.summary: A CUDA context is analogous to a CPU process, encapsulating all driver API resources and actions while maintaining distinct address spaces, thread-specific context stacks, and usage counts for interoperability.

pages.title: C++17 Features
pages.summary: nvcc supports C++17 features via the -std=c++17 flag, with specific restrictions on inline variables regarding linkage modes and structured bindings regarding memory space specifiers.

pages.title: C++20 Features
pages.summary: nvcc supports C++20 features enabled by the host compiler, with specific restrictions on modules and coroutines, while supporting the three-way comparison operator and consteval functions with execution-space caveats.

pages.title: CUDA C++ Language Restrictions and Features
pages.summary: This section details the support and restrictions for various C++ language features within CUDA, including class members, templates, const-qualified variables, attributes (deprecated, noreturn, likely/unlikely, const/pure), and specific C++11 and C++14 features such as lambda expressions, constexpr, inline namespaces, and variable templates. It also covers platform-specific constraints like those for the Microsoft and Intel host compilers.
aliases: CUDA C++ Attributes and Type Restrictions

pages.title: CUDA C++ Language Support
pages.summary: Support matrix and restrictions for C++11, C++14, C++17, and C++20 features in CUDA device code, including __CUDA_ARCH__ constraints.

pages.title: Cubemap Layered Surfaces
pages.summary: Describes cubemap layered surface access using surfCubemapLayeredread() and surfCubemapLayeredwrite() as a two-dimensional layered surface, explaining index calculation for faces across multiple cubemaps.

pages.title: Cubemap Layered Textures
pages.summary: A cubemap layered texture is a layered texture where each layer is a cubemap of identical dimensions, addressed via an integer index and three texture coordinates, allocated with specific CUDA array flags, and supported on compute capability 2.0+ devices.

pages.title: Cubemap Surfaces
pages.summary: Cubemap surfaces are accessed via surfCubemapread() and surfCubemapwrite() as a two-dimensional layered surface, using an integer index for the face and floating-point coordinates for the texel.

pages.title: Cubemap Textures
pages.summary: Cubemap textures are specialized two-dimensional layered textures comprising six equal-sized faces addressed via direction vectors, requiring compute capability 2.0 or higher and allocation via `cudaArrayCubemap`.

pages.title: CUDA Device Memory
pages.summary: Explains device memory allocation (linear memory vs CUDA arrays), address space sizes, cudaMalloc/cudaFree/cudaMemcpy usage, pitch/3D allocations, and global/constant symbol access.

pages.title: CUDA-Direct3D 12 Interoperability
pages.summary: CUDA-Direct3D 12 Interoperability enables sharing memory and synchronization objects between CUDA and D3D12 on the same physical device, supporting heaps, committed resources, buffers, mipmapped arrays, and fences via handle import and async signaling.

pages.title: Direct3D 9 Version
pages.summary: Direct3D 9 interoperability enables CUDA kernels to map, modify, and unmap Direct3D 9 resources like vertex buffers for hybrid rendering.

pages.title: Direct3D Interoperability
pages.summary: Covers Direct3D 9Ex, 10, and 11 interoperability. Specifies device creation requirements (HAL, hardware vertex processing, D3D_DRIVER_TYPE_HARDWARE). Lists registered resources (buffers, textures, surfaces) and registration functions (cudaGraphicsD3D9/10/11RegisterResource).

pages.title: CUDA Driver API
pages.summary: The CUDA Driver API is a low-level, handle-based imperative interface for managing GPU resources, contexts, and kernel execution, with all entry points prefixed by `cu`.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism
pages.summary: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism extends the programming model to enable GPU kernels to launch and synchronize with new work directly on the device, supporting nested execution and runtime data-dependent parallelism.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism API Reference
pages.summary: Overview table of CUDA Runtime API functions supported in the device runtime.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Legacy (CDP1)
pages.summary: Legacy CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) execution environment, memory model, synchronization, and programming interface.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism CDP2 vs CDP1
pages.summary: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism CDP2 introduces architectural changes over the legacy CDP1 interface, including a grid-scoped stream model, removal of explicit device synchronization, and strict 64-bit compilation requirements, while maintaining backward compatibility via specific compiler flags.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Device Management
pages.summary: Device management capabilities within the CUDA device runtime are restricted to the executing GPU, prohibiting device switching and aggregate property queries while allowing attribute inspection.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Errors
pages.summary: Error handling, API error codes, and launch failure reporting in the device runtime.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Events
pages.summary: Inter-stream synchronization capabilities of CUDA events within the device runtime.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Programming Guidelines
pages.summary: Programming guidelines, syntax, and a Hello World example for dynamic parallelism.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Launch Setup APIs
pages.summary: Low-level kernel launch APIs (`cudaGetParameterBuffer`, `cudaLaunchDevice`) exposed by the device runtime.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Limitations
pages.summary: Implementation restrictions include a configurable launch pool with default 2048 slots, device-side memory allocation limited by a heap size, volatile SM/Warp IDs due to rescheduling, and strict host-side configuration constraints.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Memory Declarations
pages.summary: Memory declarations in CUDA Dynamic Parallelism define the inheritance, accessibility, and restrictions of device, constant, texture, surface, and shared memory spaces across host-launched and device-launched kernels.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Performance
pages.summary: Performance overheads associated with dynamic parallelism-enabled kernels and system software.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism PTX Launch
pages.summary: Device-side kernel launch implementation details at the PTX level, including parameter buffer layout.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Streams
pages.summary: Streams in CUDA Dynamic Parallelism include named and unnamed (NULL) streams created with non-blocking flags, plus specialized fire-and-forget and tail launch streams for scheduling and synchronization, with strict limitations on event recording and cross-grid handle sharing.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Synchronization
pages.summary: Inter-thread and inter-grid synchronization mechanisms and limitations in the device runtime.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Toolkit Support
pages.summary: Compiler and linker support for dynamic parallelism, including automatic device runtime API inclusion and static library linking via `nvcc`.

pages.title: CUDA Events
pages.summary: CUDA Events provide a runtime mechanism to monitor device progress and perform accurate timing by asynchronously recording markers at arbitrary program points and querying their completion status.

pages.title: Extended Lambdas
pages.summary: Extended lambdas in CUDA C++ allow explicit execution space annotations within specific function scopes, supported by compile-time type traits and a namespace-scope placeholder mechanism that enforces strict scoping and addressability rules for the enclosing function.

pages.title: Mapping Buffers onto Imported Memory Objects
pages.summary: Covers mapping device pointers and mipmapped arrays onto imported NvSciBuf memory objects using `cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedBuffer` and `cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedMipmappedArray`, with strict alignment to original object attributes and mandatory cleanup via `cudaFree()` or `cudaFreeMipmappedArray()`.

pages.title: Importing and Signaling/Waiting on Imported Synchronization Objects
pages.summary: Covers importing NvSciSync objects into CUDA as external semaphores via cudaImportExternalSemaphore and managing synchronization through asynchronous signaling and waiting operations, including cache coherency flag configuration.

pages.title: Floating-Point Standard
pages.summary: NVIDIA compute devices adhere to the IEEE 754-2008 standard with specific deviations regarding rounding modes, exception masking, NaN propagation, flush-to-zero behavior in atomics, and integer conversion rules.

pages.title: Graphics Interoperability
pages.summary: Graphics Interoperability enables mapping OpenGL and Direct3D resources into the CUDA address space to facilitate bidirectional data exchange between graphics APIs and CUDA kernels.

pages.title: CUDA Graphs
pages.summary: CUDA Graphs provide a work submission model that separates graph definition, instantiation, and execution to reduce CPU launch overhead and enable system-level optimizations unavailable with piecewise stream submission.

pages.title: CUDA Kernel Execution
pages.summary: Details the cuLaunchKernel API, parameter passing mechanisms (array of pointers vs extra options buffer), and strict alignment requirements for device code types, structures, and CUdeviceptr.

pages.title: Layered Textures
pages.summary: Explains one-dimensional and two-dimensional layered textures (texture arrays), addressing using integer layer indices and floating-point coordinates, CUDA array creation with cudaArray-Layered, fetch functions, and compute capability 2.0+ requirement.

pages.title: Maximizing CUDA Utilization
pages.summary: Maximizing CUDA utilization involves exposing parallelism across host, device, and multiprocessor levels while hiding latency through sufficient warp residency, constrained by on-chip register and shared memory limits.

pages.title: CUDA Mode Switches
pages.summary: Explains how display mode switches (resolution/bit depth changes, fullscreen app launches, task switches) alter the memory required for the GPU's primary surface. Notes that if mode switches increase primary surface memory, CUDA may cannibalize allocations, causing subsequent CUDA runtime calls to fail with an invalid context error.

pages.title: CUDA Module
pages.summary: Modules are dynamically loadable packages of device code and data, analogous to Windows DLLs, that enable third-party interoperability within a CUDA context through maintained symbol scope and runtime loading capabilities.

pages.title: Polymorphic Function Wrappers (nvstd::function)
pages.summary: nvstd::function is a polymorphic function wrapper class template provided in the nvfunctional header, enabling the storage, copying, and invocation of callable targets in both host and device code, subject to strict execution space initialization and passing restrictions.

pages.title: CUDA Occupancy Calculator
pages.summary: The CUDA Occupancy Calculator APIs assist programmers in selecting thread block and cluster sizes based on register and shared memory constraints to predict occupancy and optimize multiprocessor utilization.

pages.title: OpenGL Interoperability
pages.summary: OpenGL-CUDA interoperability enables direct mapping of OpenGL buffer, texture, and renderbuffer objects into CUDA's address space for unified memory access across graphics and compute workloads.

pages.title: CUDA Overview & Scalable Programming Model
pages.summary: CUDA is a general-purpose parallel computing platform and programming model introduced by NVIDIA that leverages GPU architecture for massive parallelism, utilizing a scalable model based on thread hierarchies, shared memory, and barrier synchronization to automatically scale across diverse hardware.

pages.title: cuda::pipeline API
pages.summary: The cuda::pipeline API is a C++ synchronization object that manages a double-ended FIFO queue to overlap asynchronous data transfers with computation.

pages.title: CUDA Pipeline Primitives Interface
pages.summary: The CUDA Pipeline Primitives Interface provides a C-compatible API for asynchronous global-to-shared memory transfers, featuring submission, batching, synchronization, and barrier integration primitives alongside strict alignment and race-condition constraints.

pages.title: CUDA Pointer Attributes
pages.summary: CUDA pointer attributes provide runtime metadata about how memory was allocated or registered, enabling applications to distinguish between managed, host, device, and unregistered memory types without tracking physical residency.

pages.title: CUDA Programming Interface & NVCC Compilation
pages.summary: The CUDA programming interface consists of C++ language extensions and a runtime library, compiled via nvcc which separates host and device code into PTX/cubin binaries and modified host code.

pages.title: CUDA Programming Model
pages.summary: The CUDA programming model defines the parallel execution architecture, thread hierarchy, memory spaces, and hardware abstraction for GPU-accelerated computing.

pages.title: Read/Write Coherency
pages.summary: Texture and surface memory are cached and not kept coherent with global or surface writes within the same kernel call, causing reads to return undefined data if the location was written to in the same kernel call; safe reads require updates from previous kernel calls or memory copies.

pages.title: CUDA Runtime
pages.summary: The CUDA Runtime is implemented in the cudart library, providing a programming interface for device memory management, asynchronous execution, and graphics interoperability while assuming separate host and device memory spaces.

pages.title: CUDA Runtime and Driver API Interoperability
pages.summary: Explains how runtime and driver APIs can be mixed. Driver-created contexts are picked up by runtime calls, and vice versa. Memory allocated via one API can be cast and used by the other.

pages.title: CUDA Runtime Initialization
pages.summary: CUDA Runtime initialization manages global state and primary contexts, shifting in CUDA 12.0 to explicit initialization via cudaSetDevice() and cudaInitDevice(), while warning against API usage during program startup or teardown.

pages.title: CUDA Stream-Ordered Memory Addendums
pages.summary: This page documents specific driver-level behaviors and constraints for the CUDA stream-ordered memory allocator, including context requirements for async memcpy, undefined behavior for post-free attribute queries, graph memset incompatibilities, pointer attribute behaviors, and CPU virtual memory limitations.

pages.title: CUDA Streams
pages.summary: Ordered sequences of commands (kernels, copies) that enable concurrent execution across multiple streams. Covers stream lifecycle, dependency management, and resource cleanup behavior.

pages.title: Surface Memory
pages.summary: Introduces surface memory for compute capability 2.0+ devices, allowing read/write access to CUDA arrays via surface objects. References Table 27 for maximum surface dimensions.

pages.title: Surface Object API
pages.summary: The Surface Object API enables read/write access to CUDA arrays via surface objects created from resource descriptions, utilizing byte-addressing for coordinates and requiring the cudaArraySurfaceLoadStore flag during array allocation.

pages.title: Tesla Compute Cluster (TCC) Mode
pages.summary: TCC mode is a Windows device driver configuration for Tesla and Quadro Series GPUs, accessible via nvidia-smi, that removes all graphics functionality.

pages.title: Texture Fetching
pages.summary: Texture fetching computes returned values based on texture object attributes, covering coordinate systems, nearest-point sampling, linear filtering, and table lookup implementations.

pages.title: Texture Gather
pages.summary: Details the tex2Dgather() function for 2D textures, which returns four 32-bit values for a specific component from the four texels used in bilinear filtering. Covers 8-bit fractional precision limitations, cudaArrayTextureGather flag requirements, size limits, and compute capability 2.0+ requirement.

pages.title: Time Function
pages.summary: The Time Function provides device-side functions that return the value of a per-multiprocessor counter incremented every clock cycle, enabling measurement of the total clock cycles a thread takes to execute within a kernel, which includes scheduling overhead due to time-slicing.

pages.title: CUDA Versioning and Compatibility
pages.summary: Explains the two key version numbers in CUDA development: compute capability (device features) and driver API version. Details backward compatibility (applications compiled against older API versions work on newer drivers) and lack of forward compatibility. Outlines rules for mixing driver/runtime versions and static linking of CUDA Toolkit libraries.

pages.title: CUDA-Vulkan Interoperability
pages.summary: Covers the interoperability between CUDA and Vulkan, including extent conversion, memory object importing, synchronization object importing, and signaling/waiting operations.

pages.title: 10.43. Custom ABI Pragmas
pages.summary: The #pragma nv_abi directive optimizes separate compilation performance by allowing developers to modify custom Application Binary Interface (ABI) properties, such as register preservation limits, for device functions.

pages.title: Data Migration and Coherency on Pre-6.x Architectures
pages.summary: Explains the lack of fine-grained data movement on pre-6.x GPUs, the introduction of the GPU page faulting mechanism in CC 6.x, and system-wide virtual address space benefits.

pages.title: Data Prefetching in CUDA
pages.summary: Describes cudaMemPrefetchAsync, an asynchronous stream-ordered API to migrate data closer to a specified processor (GPU or CPU), supporting both system-allocated and managed memory workflows within the Unified Memory performance optimization suite.

pages.title: Data Usage Hints in CUDA
pages.summary: cudaMemAdvise provides performance-only hints to the CUDA Unified Memory driver regarding access patterns, preferred locations, and frequent accessors to optimize data placement and migration across multiple processors.

pages.title: Default Stream
pages.summary: The default stream processes operations lacking an explicit stream parameter in sequential order, with its scope defined as either a shared device-wide NULL stream or independent per-thread streams based on the --default-stream compilation flag.

pages.title: Device Enumeration
pages.summary: Host systems can contain multiple CUDA-enabled devices, requiring enumeration to determine the device count and query attributes such as compute capability using cudaGetDeviceCount and cudaGetDeviceProperties.

pages.title: Device Graph Launch Overview
pages.summary: Covers the concept of device graphs, which allow dynamic control flow and decision-making directly on the GPU without host round-trips. Requires unified addressing. Distinguishes between device graphs (launchable from host/device) and host graphs (host-only). Notes concurrency restrictions: device graphs cannot be launched twice from the device simultaneously, and simultaneous host/device launches cause undefined behavior.

pages.title: Device Graph Requirements
pages.summary: Device graphs impose strict constraints on node locality, allowed node types, kernel execution modes, and memory accessibility to ensure valid instantiation and launch from the GPU.

pages.title: Device Launch Modes
pages.summary: Device graphs require distinct named streams rather than regular CUDA streams, with three specific modes available: Fire and Forget, Tail Launch, and Sibling Launch.

pages.title: Device Memory Accesses
pages.summary: Explains how warp-level memory accesses across different CUDA addressable spaces are coalesced, aligned, cached, and how these factors impact instruction throughput and bandwidth.

pages.title: Device Memory Space Specifiers
pages.summary: Restrictions and rules for device memory space specifiers, including constructor/destructor constraints and compilation mode differences.

pages.title: Device Selection
pages.summary: Host threads can set the active device using cudaSetDevice(). All memory allocations and kernel launches occur on the currently set device. If unset, device 0 is used by default.

pages.title: Device-Side Tensor Map Encoding and Modification
pages.summary: This page details the process of creating, copying, and modifying tiled-type tensor maps on the device using `cuda::ptx::tensormap_replace` functions, along with the release-acquire synchronization pattern required for cross-block usage.

pages.title: 10.42. Diagnostic Pragmas
pages.summary: Diagnostic pragmas enable control over compiler message severity within the nvcc frontend by suppressing, warning, or promoting specific diagnostics, managing state via push/pop, and enforcing the use of nv_-prefixed variants in CUDA 12.0+.

pages.title: Direct Unified Memory Access from Host
pages.summary: Direct Unified Memory Access from Host enables hardware-coherent reads, stores, and atomics from the host to GPU-resident memory without page faults, requiring the cudaDevAttrDirectManagedMemAccessFromHost attribute and cudaMemAdviseSetAccessedBy hint.

pages.title: Direct3D 10 Interoperability
pages.summary: Demonstrates how to create a Direct3D 10 device, register a vertex buffer with CUDA, map it for GPU writing, launch a kernel to generate vertex data, and unmap it for rendering.

pages.title: Direct3D 11 Buffer Mapping
pages.summary: Maps device pointers onto imported Direct3D 11 memory objects using cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedBuffer.

pages.title: Direct3D 11 Device LUID Matching
pages.summary: Matches CUDA devices to Direct3D 11 devices by comparing their Local Unique Identifiers (LUIDs).

pages.title: Direct3D 11 Interoperability
pages.summary: Direct3D 11 interoperability enables CUDA kernels to access Direct3D 11 resources by registering the resource, mapping it to CUDA memory, executing the kernel, and unmapping the resource.

pages.title: Direct3D 11 Memory Object Import
pages.summary: Imports shareable Direct3D 11 textures and buffers into CUDA using NT handles, named handles, or KMT handles.

pages.title: Direct3D 11 Mipmapped Array Mapping
pages.summary: Maps CUDA mipmapped arrays onto imported Direct3D 11 memory objects, bridging DXGI formats, texture extents, and bind flags through dedicated conversion utilities.

pages.title: Direct3D 11 Synchronization Object Import
pages.summary: Imports shareable Direct3D 11 fence and keyed mutex synchronization objects into CUDA using NT or KMT handles.

pages.title: Direct3D 11 Synchronization Signaling and Waiting
pages.summary: Signals and waits on imported Direct3D 11 fence and keyed mutex objects using CUDA asynchronous semaphore APIs.

pages.title: Distributed Shared Memory
pages.summary: Shared memory partitioned across thread blocks within a cluster (compute capability 9.0+), enabling cross-block read/write/atomic operations via cooperative_groups API.

pages.title: CUDA Driver Entry Point Access
pages.summary: Provides APIs (cuGetProcAddress, cudaGetDriverEntryPointByVersion) to retrieve driver function addresses dynamically. Covers versioned typedefs, ABI compatibility, per-thread default stream variants, and accessing newer driver features without toolkit updates.

pages.title: Dynamic Global Memory Allocation and Operations
pages.summary: Dynamic global memory allocation is supported on compute capability 2.x and higher. Functions include __device__ malloc(), __nv_aligned_device_malloc(), free(), memcpy(), and memset(). Memory is allocated from a fixed-size device heap (defa

pages.title: Error Checking
pages.summary: Runtime functions return error codes, but asynchronous functions report errors via subsequent calls. cudaDeviceSynchronize() is required to catch async errors. cudaPeekAtLastError() and cudaGetLastError() manage thread-local error variables. Kernel launches require explicit error checking immediately after launch, and cudaErrorNotReady is not considered an error.

pages.title: Exception Handling
pages.summary: Exception handling supported only in host code; not supported for __global__ functions.

pages.title: 10.37. Execution Configuration
pages.summary: Covers the execution configuration syntax for __global__ functions, including grid/block dimensions, shared memory allocation, stream association, and cluster dimensions (CC 9.0+).

pages.title: Explicit Synchronization
pages.summary: Methods to explicitly synchronize streams include cudaDeviceSynchronize(), cudaStreamSynchronize(), cudaStreamWaitEvent(), and cudaStreamQuery().

pages.title: Explicit Synchronization and Logical GPU Activity
pages.summary: Details how Unified Memory uses logical activity to determine GPU idle state, valid synchronization functions, stream dependencies, and constraints on concurrent CPU/GPU access.

pages.title: Extended GPU Memory
pages.summary: CUDA feature enabling GPUs to access system memory via NVLink-C2C/NVSwitch, supporting single-node and multi-node topologies with VMM and Memory Pool APIs.

pages.title: Extended Lambda Restrictions
pages.summary: Detailed restrictions on CUDA extended lambdas, including enclosing function constraints, template parameter rules, capture variable limitations, `__CUDA_ARCH__` independence, placeholder type behavior, type trait mismatches, and diagnostic expectations.

pages.title: External Linkage
pages.summary: Rules for calling functions declared with extern qualifier in device code.

pages.title: External Resource Interoperability
pages.summary: Introduces the framework for importing external memory and synchronization objects (semaphores) from other APIs using OS-native handles or NVIDIA SWI. Covers cudaImportExternalMemory(), cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedBuffer(), cudaImportExternalSemaphore(), and their lifecycle management.

pages.title: Fabric Memory
pages.summary: Introduces CU_MEM_HANDLE_TYPE_FABRIC for intra-node and inter-node memory sharing via NVIDIA IMEX daemon and NVLINK fabric.

pages.title: Formatted Output
pages.summary: Formatted output is supported on compute capability 2.x and higher via the in-kernel printf() function. It behaves similarly to the standard C library printf() but outputs to a host-side stream. Each thread executes printf independently. Th

pages.title: Friend Functions
pages.summary: Restrictions on defining __global__ functions or templates in friend declarations.

pages.title: Function Parameters
pages.summary: Limits on __global__ function parameters (32,764 bytes on Volta+, 4 KB older), restrictions on variable arguments and pass-by-reference, and ODR requirements.

pages.title: Function Pointers
pages.summary: Restrictions on taking addresses of __global__ and __device__ functions across host/device boundaries.

pages.title: Function Recursion
pages.summary: __global__ functions do not support recursion.

pages.title: __global__ Function Argument Processing
pages.summary: Behavior of __global__ function arguments from host code: memcpy instead of copy constructors, and potential early destructor invocation due to asynchronous execution.

pages.title: GPU Memory Oversubscription
pages.summary: Explains how Unified Memory allows applications to allocate and share arrays larger than individual processor memory capacities, enabling out-of-core processing without significant model complexity.

pages.title: GPU Memory Oversubscription on Pre-6.x
pages.summary: States the limitation that devices with compute capability lower than 6.0 cannot allocate more managed memory than the physical size of GPU memory.

pages.title: Graph API Usage Guidelines
pages.summary: Covers thread-safety rules for `cudaGraph_t` (not thread-safe, user responsibility), concurrency limits for `cudaGraphExec_t` (cannot run concurrently with itself, ordered launches), and the role of streams for ordering without constraining internal graph parallelism.

pages.title: Graph Execution Environments
pages.summary: Covers the hierarchical execution environment model for device-side synchronization. Environments encapsulate graph work and generated fire-and-forget work. Host launches create a parent stream environment. Completion is marked when the graph and all child work finish.

pages.title: Graph Memory Footprint
pages.summary: Management of physical memory footprint for graphs, including trimming via cudaDeviceGraphMemTrim and querying attributes.

pages.title: Graph Memory Nodes
pages.summary: Explains how graphs manage GPU-ordered memory allocations, their lifetimes, API fundamentals, stream capture, and cross-graph access/freeing procedures.

pages.title: Graph Memory Performance
pages.summary: Performance considerations for graph memory, including remapping costs and stream consistency.

pages.title: Graph Peer Access
pages.summary: Configuration of graph allocations for multi-GPU peer access and virtual address reuse across GPUs.

pages.title: Grid Synchronization
pages.summary: Covers grid-level synchronization in CUDA, including the grid.sync() function, cooperative kernel launch requirements, occupancy calculation for maximizing parallelism, and device/platform compatibility constraints.

pages.title: Hardware Multithreading
pages.summary: Explains on-chip context maintenance for warps, enabling zero-cost context switching. Details partitioning of registers and shared memory among warps/blocks, limits on resident blocks/warps, and the formula for calculating total warps per block. Notes that insufficient resources cause kernel launch failures.

pages.title: Hardware vs. Software Coherency in Page Tables
pages.summary: Contrasts hardware coherent systems (combined CPU/GPU page tables) with software coherent systems (separate page tables requiring page faults for coherency). Explains performance benefits of hardware coherency regarding TLB misses, cache-line granularity contention, and atomic updates.

pages.title: Host-Device Data Transfer
pages.summary: Details techniques to minimize host-device data transfers, including batching small transfers, using page-locked host memory, mapped memory, and handling integrated systems.

pages.title: Host Device Lambda Notes
pages.summary: Behavioral notes on `__host__ __device__` lambdas, specifically regarding indirect function calls and host compiler inlining limitations.

pages.title: Host Functions (Callbacks)
pages.summary: cudaLaunchHostFunc() inserts a CPU function call into a stream that executes after preceding commands complete. Commands issued after a callback wait for it, and callbacks must not make CUDA API calls to avoid deadlock.

pages.title: Host Native Atomics
pages.summary: Describes hardware-accelerated atomic accesses to CPU-resident memory on NVLink-connected coherent systems, eliminating the need for page fault emulation.

pages.title: Implicit Synchronization
pages.summary: Operations from different streams cannot run concurrently if a CUDA operation on the NULL stream is submitted between them, unless non-blocking streams are used. Guidelines recommend issuing independent operations first and delaying synchronization.

pages.title: Implicitly-declared and non-virtual explicitly-defaulted functions
pages.summary: Rules for determining execution space specifiers (__host__, __device__) for implicitly-declared or defaulted functions based on callers.

pages.title: Inter-Process Communication with Unified Memory
pages.summary: Explains that CUDA IPC does not support Managed Memory, but System-Allocated Memory on full-support systems is IPC-capable. Lists Linux mechanisms (mmap MAP_SHARED, POSIX IPC, memfd_create) for sharing memory across processes.

pages.title: Interprocess Communication
pages.summary: CUDA IPC API allows sharing device memory pointers and events across processes using cudaIpcGetMemHandle() and cudaIpcOpenMemHandle(). Supports 64-bit Linux and compute capability 2.0+. Memory sharing may expose sub-allocations, so 2MiB aligned allocations are recommended. L4T/Tegra devices have limited IPC support (events only for compute 7.x+).

pages.title: IPC Memory Pools
pages.summary: Covers the creation, sharing, access control, and limitations of IPC-capable memory pools in CUDA, including handle export/import, setting access permissions, and sharing specific allocations across processes.

pages.title: Just-in-Time Compilation
pages.summary: Explains how PTX code is compiled to binary at runtime by the device driver, the benefits of JIT compilation, the compute cache mechanism, and its invalidation policy.

pages.title: L2 Cache Access Management
pages.summary: Describes L2 cache set-aside for persisting accesses, access policy windows, hit ratios, access properties (streaming, persisting, normal), and API functions for querying and controlling L2 cache behavior.

pages.title: 10.38. Launch Bounds
pages.summary: Covers the __launch_bounds__() qualifier, its parameters, compiler heuristics for register usage optimization, and architecture-specific handling via __CUDA_ARCH__.

pages.title: Lazy Loading in CUDA
pages.summary: Covers the entire Chapter 25 on Lazy Loading, including its purpose, version support (Driver/Toolkit/Compiler), triggering mechanisms, querying the loading mode, and potential issues like concurrent execution deadlocks, VRAM allocation conflicts, and autotuning warmup requirements.

pages.title: Load Functions Using Cache Hints
pages.summary: Reference for cache-hinted load intrinsics (__ldcg, __ldca, __ldcs, __ldlu, __ldcv) that control cache behavior for global memory reads, supporting various data types and requiring compute capability 5.0 or higher.

pages.title: Managed Memory Direct Access & System Allocator vs Managed Memory Behavior
pages.summary: Covers the difference between system-allocated memory and managed memory, and how the directManagedMemAccessFromHost hardware feature affects CPU/GPU memory migration and faulting behavior.

pages.title: __managed__ Memory Space Specifier
pages.summary: Restrictions and examples for __managed__ variables, including const/reference limitations, runtime state dependencies, and multi-GPU allocation behavior.

pages.title: Unified Memory on Windows or Compute Capability 5.x
pages.summary: Covers Managed Memory v1.0 support on Windows and devices with compute capability lower than 6.0, highlighting limited data migration, coherency, and oversubscription support.

pages.title: Mathematical Functions
pages.summary: Introductory reference to C/C++ standard library mathematical functions and CUDA device intrinsic mathematical functions supported in device code.

pages.title: Mathematical Functions Accuracy
pages.summary: Accuracy bounds (ULP errors) for standard and intrinsic mathematical functions across single, double, and quad precision.

pages.title: Maximize Instruction Throughput
pages.summary: Brief section directing readers to the CUDA C++ Best Practices Guide for instruction throughput optimization details.

pages.title: Maximize Memory Throughput
pages.summary: Outlines the strategy for maximizing memory throughput by minimizing low-bandwidth transfers, leveraging on-chip memory (shared memory, L1/L2 caches), and optimizing global memory access patterns for coalescing.

pages.title: 10.39. Maximum Number of Registers per Thread
pages.summary: Covers the __maxnreg__() qualifier for limiting register allocation per thread, its incompatibility with __launch_bounds__(), and the maxrregcount compiler option.

pages.title: Memory Discarding in CUDA
pages.summary: Details cudaMemDiscardBatchAsync and cudaMemDiscardAndPrefetchBatchAsync APIs. Explains how marking memory ranges as discarded prevents redundant migrations during faults/evictions, improving performance. Includes important considerations regarding indeterminate reads and concurrent access.

pages.title: Memory Fences and Synchronization Example
pages.summary: Code example and explanation demonstrating the use of __threadfence(), atomic operations, and __syncthreads() to coordinate block completion and ensure memory visibility across threads.

pages.title: Mapping Memory
pages.summary: Details cuMemMap to associate physical allocations with reserved virtual address ranges, and cuMemUnmap for decoupling.

pages.title: Memory Paging and Page Sizes
pages.summary: Defines virtual vs. physical pages, page sizes (4KiB, 2MiB, etc.), page tables, and TLBs. Explains how virtual addressing and paging mechanics impact Unified Memory performance tuning.

pages.title: Memory Synchronization Domains
pages.summary: Hopper architecture (CUDA 12.0+) feature that isolates memory traffic into logical domains to reduce fence/flush interference. Uses launch attributes to map logical domains to physical domains and manage cross-domain system-scope fencing.

pages.title: Memory Type (CUDA 10.2+)
pages.summary: Introduction to user-controlled memory allocation via cuMemCreate and CUmemAllocationProp::allocFlags.

pages.title: Minimize Memory Thrashing
pages.summary: Provides recommendations for managing device memory allocation to avoid performance degradation, including sizing allocations, batching cudaMalloc/cudaFree calls, and using cudaMallocManaged for oversubscription.

pages.title: Multi-GPU Managed Memory on Pre-6.x
pages.summary: Details managed memory visibility across GPUs via peer-to-peer, Linux driver migration behavior, Windows zero-copy fallback, and the CUDA_MANAGED_FORCE_DEVICE_ALLOC environment variable.

pages.title: Multicast Support
pages.summary: Details NVLINK SHARP support via Multicast Objects, cuMulticastCreate, cuMulticastAddDevice, and multimem PTX instructions for broadcast/reductions.

pages.title: Namespace Reservations
pages.summary: Rules prohibiting additions to reserved CUDA namespaces, with examples of valid/invalid usage.

pages.title: Nanosleep Function
pages.summary: Verbatim source-backed fallback page for Nanosleep Function.

pages.title: NVIDIA Software Communication Interface (NVSCI) Interoperability
pages.summary: Allocates, configures, and imports NvSciBuf memory objects into CUDA, including cache and compression attribute handling.

pages.title: Occupancy API and Calculator
pages.summary: Covers the occupancy-based kernel launch API (cudaOccupancyMaxPotentialBlockSize), cluster occupancy API (cudaOccupancyMaxPotentialClusterSize, cudaOccupancyMaxActiveClusters), and the standalone Nsight Compute occupancy calculator tool.

pages.title: Optimized Memory Reuse
pages.summary: CUDA reuses memory via virtual address assignment within graphs and physical memory aliasing between graphs.

pages.title: Page-Locked Host Memory
pages.summary: Host memory allocated with cudaHostAlloc or cudaHostRegister that prevents paging, enabling concurrent transfers, device mapping, and higher bandwidth. Includes portable, write-combining, and mapped memory variants.

pages.title: Peer-to-Peer Memory Access
pages.summary: Devices can directly address each other's memory based on PCIe/NVLINK topology. Enabled via cudaDeviceEnablePeerAccess() and verified with cudaDeviceCanAccessPeer(). Requires 64-bit applications and unified address space. Linux bare-metal systems require IOMMU to be disabled for PCIe P2P, while VM pass-through with VFIO is supported.

pages.title: Peer-to-Peer Memory Copy
pages.summary: Memory can be copied directly between devices using cudaMemcpyPeer() or similar functions. Copies in the implicit NULL stream synchronize with all previous commands. Asynchronous copies can overlap with other operations. P2P access via cudaDeviceEnablePeerAccess() avoids staging through host memory, improving performance.

pages.title: Unified Memory Performance Tuning
pages.summary: Provides general advice for tuning Unified Memory performance: understanding paging, keeping data local, tuning for transfer granularity, and carefully using performance hints to avoid overhead.

pages.title: Pointers
pages.summary: Rules for pointer dereferencing across host/device boundaries and usage of addresses for device/shared/constant variables.

pages.title: 10.40. #pragma unroll
pages.summary: Covers the #pragma unroll directive for controlling loop unrolling behavior, including ICE arguments and compiler fallback rules.

pages.title: Profiler Counter Function
pages.summary: Each multiprocessor has sixteen hardware counters that applications can increment via the __prof_trigger() function. Counters 0-7 are available for applications, while 8-15 are reserved. Values can be retrieved using nvprof --events prof_tr

pages.title: Programmatic Dependent Launch
pages.summary: A mechanism (compute capability 9.0+) allowing a secondary kernel to launch before its primary kernel finishes in the same stream. Uses cudaTriggerProgrammaticLaunchCompletion() and cudaGridDependencySynchronize() for safe concurrent execution. Supported in CUDA Graphs via edge data.

pages.title: Querying Data Usage Attributes
pages.summary: Describes cudaMemRangeGetAttribute and cudaMemRangeGetAttributes APIs used to query attributes set via cudaMemAdvise or cudaMemPrefetchAsync. Lists queryable attributes like ReadMostly, PreferredLocation, AccessedBy, and LastPrefetchLocation.

pages.title: Relaxed Constexpr
pages.summary: Experimental `-expt-relaxed-constexpr` flag enabling cross-execution space constexpr calls (host-to-device and device-to-host) when evaluated in constant-evaluation contexts.

pages.title: Run Time Type Information (RTTI)
pages.summary: RTTI features supported only in host code, not device code.

pages.title: Runtime Detection of Unified Memory Support Level
pages.summary: Provides a code example demonstrating how to query device attributes (cudaDevAttrPageableMemoryAccess, cudaDevAttrConcurrentManagedAccess) at runtime to determine the level of Unified Memory support available on the system.

pages.title: Shared Memory
pages.summary: Explains shared memory allocation using __shared__, its performance benefits over global memory, and provides detailed matrix multiplication examples demonstrating shared memory usage for tiling and synchronization.

pages.title: 10.41. SIMD Video Instructions
pages.summary: Covers PTX ISA 3.0 SIMD video instructions, their availability on CC 3.0+, and the syntax for inline assembly using the asm() statement.

pages.title: SIMT Architecture
pages.summary: Details the Single-Instruction, Multiple-Thread (SIMT) execution model. Explains warps (32 threads), warp scheduling, branch divergence handling, and the shift from pre-Volta shared program counters to Volta's Independent Thread Scheduling (per-thread state, sub-warp divergence/reconvergence). Covers active/inactive thread states and memory write/atomic behavior within warps.

pages.title: SLI Interoperability
pages.summary: Explains special considerations for CUDA interoperability in SLI configurations, including memory allocation impacts across GPUs, the recommendation to create one CUDA context per GPU, and using cudaD3D[9|10|11]GetDevices() or cudaGLGetDevices() to map resources to the correct device per frame.

pages.title: Standard Library
pages.summary: Standard libraries supported only in host code unless specified otherwise.

pages.title: Static Variables within Function
pages.summary: Rules for memory space specifiers of static variables in device/host functions, including initialization restrictions and __CUDA_ARCH__ conditions.

pages.title: Store Functions Using Cache Hints
pages.summary: Reference for cache-hinted store intrinsics (__stwb, __stcg, __stcs, __stwt) that control cache behavior for global memory writes, supporting various data types and requiring compute capability 5.0 or higher.

pages.title: Stream and Event Behavior
pages.summary: Details the behavior of streams and events across different devices. Kernel launches fail if issued to a stream not associated with the current device, while memory copies may succeed. Events and streams on different devices have specific synchronization and error-checking rules. Default streams on different devices may execute concurrently.

pages.title: Memcpy()/Memset() Behavior With Stream-associated Unified Memory
pages.summary: Details the rules for cudaMemcpy*() and cudaMemset*() operations on stream-associated unified memory, including coherency checks, stream activity constraints, and error conditions.

pages.title: Managing Data Visibility with cudaStreamAttachMemAsync
pages.summary: Explains how cudaStreamAttachMemAsync() associates managed allocations with specific streams to enable finer-grained CPU/GPU concurrency and data transfer optimizations.

pages.title: Stream Attach With Multithreaded Host Programs
pages.summary: Describes using cudaStreamAttachMemAsync() for independent task parallelism in multi-threaded programs, the role of the cudaMemAttachHost flag, and modular program constraints.

pages.title: Stream Association Examples and Visibility Rules
pages.summary: Provides code examples demonstrating stream attachment, host-attach flags, and explains the conservative default global visibility behavior when allocations are not stream-attached.

pages.title: Stream Capture Peer Access
pages.summary: Behavior of peer accessibility recording during stream capture, where pool access state at capture time is fixed.

pages.title: Stream Ordered Memory Allocator
pages.summary: Covers the Stream Ordered Memory Allocator (CUDA 11.2+), including cudaMallocAsync/cudaFreeAsync, memory pools, caching behavior, reuse policies, and multi-GPU accessibility.

pages.title: Stream Overlap Behavior
pages.summary: Execution overlap between streams depends on command order and device support for overlapping data transfers and kernel execution. Proper ordering can hide launch latency and enable concurrent execution.

pages.title: Stream Priorities
pages.summary: Stream priorities can be set at creation using cudaStreamCreateWithPriority(). The GPU scheduler uses these as hints to prioritize higher-priority tasks, but they do not preempt running tasks or guarantee strict ordering.

pages.title: Surface Functions
pages.summary: Reference for CUDA surface read/write functions (surf1Dread, surf1Dwrite, surf2Dread, surf2Dwrite, surf3Dread, surf3Dwrite, and their layered and cubemap variants), including boundary mode handling and coordinate specifications.

pages.title: Synchronization API Actions
pages.summary: Details how the CUDA driver synchronizes and releases memory based on stream-ordered allocation policies, including checking release thresholds and making freed allocations available.

pages.title: Synchronization Functions
pages.summary: Documentation for CUDA synchronization primitives including __syncthreads(), __syncthreads_count(), __syncthreads_and(), __syncthreads_or(), and __syncwarp(), detailing their behavior, usage constraints, and memory ordering guarantees.

pages.title: Synchronous Calls
pages.summary: Synchronous functions block the host thread until the device completes the requested task. Host thread behavior (yield, block, or spin) can be configured using cudaSetDeviceFlags() before any CUDA calls.

pages.title: System-Allocated Memory Examples
pages.summary: Demonstrates advanced use-cases for System-Allocated Memory on devices with full Unified Memory support. Includes examples for malloc, cudaMallocManaged, stack variables, file-scope/static variables, global-scope variables, extern variables, and file-backed memory (mmap).

pages.title: Tensor Map Alignment and Properties
pages.summary: Covers negative indices, out-of-bounds handling, size/stride definitions, and alignment requirements for multi-dimensional bulk tensor asynchronous copy operations.

pages.title: Asynchronous Data Copies using the Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA)
pages.summary: The Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA) is a hardware feature introduced in Compute Capability 9.0 that provides an efficient mechanism for asynchronous data transfers between global memory, shared memory, and distributed shared memory. It supports both one-dimensional contiguous arrays and multi-dimensional arrays (up to 5D) via tensor maps, reducing the need for error-prone address calculations on the device. TMA operations are asynchronous, allowing the CPU/GPU to continue computation while data is transferred, and utilize specific completion mechanisms like shared memory barriers (mbarrier) and bulk async-groups.

pages.title: Texture and Surface Memory
pages.summary: CUDA provides access to texture and surface memory for performance benefits over global memory. Texture objects specify texture dimensions, texel types, read modes, coordinate normalization, addressing modes, and filtering modes (point or linear). Textures support 1D, 2D, and 3D addressing with various coordinate transformations and interpolation methods.

pages.title: Texture Functions
pages.summary: Comprehensive reference for CUDA texture fetching functions (tex1Dfetch, tex1D, tex2D, tex3D, texCubemap, and their layered, gradient, LOD, and sparse array variants), detailing parameters, coordinate systems, and addressing modes.

pages.title: Texture Object API
pages.summary: Texture objects are created using cudaCreateTextureObject() with resource and texture descriptions. The API supports advanced features like anisotropic filtering, mipmapping, and sRGB. The section provides a complete code example demonstrating texture allocation, configuration, kernel execution, and cleanup.

pages.title: This Capture By Value
pages.summary: C++17 `*this` capture mode for lambdas in CUDA, solving host memory access runtime errors by copying the object instead of capturing the pointer. Covers supported contexts and dialect requirements.

pages.title: TMA Swizzle Patterns
pages.summary: Explains the use of swizzle patterns (32B, 64B, 128B, NONE) to rearrange data in shared memory, avoiding bank conflicts during matrix transpose and other access patterns. Includes offset formulas and alignment requirements.

pages.title: Toolkit and Driver Compatibility
pages.summary: Requirements for 12.1 Toolkit/r530 driver+ for kernels with >4KB parameters, and link compatibility rules across toolkit revisions.

pages.title: Trap Function
pages.summary: The __trap() function can be called from any device thread to initiate a trap operation. Execution of the kernel is aborted and an interrupt is raised in the host program.

pages.title: Unified Memory Atomic Accesses & Synchronization Primitives
pages.summary: Covers CUDA Unified Memory support for atomic operations, heterogeneous synchronization primitives, and page fault behavior on systems with/without CPU and GPU page tables.

pages.title: Unified Memory on Devices with Compute Capability 6.x+
pages.summary: Describes full support for CUDA Managed Memory on devices with compute capability 6.x or higher but without pageable memory access, noting the exclusion of system allocators.

pages.title: Memcpy()/Memset() Behavior With Unified Memory
pages.summary: Explains how cudaMemcpy*() and cudaMemset*() accept unified memory pointers, the role of cudaMemcpyKind as a performance hint, and best practices for performance optimization.

pages.title: Unified Memory Performance Hints
pages.summary: Introduces performance hints (cudaMemPrefetchAsync, cudaMemAdvise, etc.) that allow applications to provide CUDA with additional information to optimize data placement and migration without altering application semantics.

pages.title: Difference between Unified Memory and Mapped Memory
pages.summary: Explains the core difference: Mapped Memory lacks guaranteed support for all memory operations (e.g., atomics) across systems, while Unified Memory guarantees portability for these operations.

pages.title: Unified Virtual Address Space
pages.summary: In 64-bit processes, a single address space covers the host and all devices (compute capability 2.0+). Pointers can be queried for location using cudaPointerGetAttributes(), and cudaMemcpyDefault can be used for transfers. cudaHostAlloc() creates portable memory accessible directly by kernels without explicit device pointer conversion.

pages.title: Reserving a Virtual Address Range
pages.summary: Explains cuMemAddressReserve for carving out virtual address space independent of physical memory, analogous to mmap/VirtualAlloc.

pages.title: Virtual Aliasing Support
pages.summary: Describes creating multiple virtual memory mappings (proxies) to the same allocation via cuMemMap, and handling coherence/fencing with fence.proxy.alias.

pages.title: Virtual Memory Management
pages.summary: CUDA Virtual Memory Management (VMM) APIs for fine-grained control over the unified virtual address space, including physical memory allocation, VA range reservation, mapping, and interprocess communication.

pages.title: Volatile Qualifier
pages.summary: Limitations of the volatile keyword in CUDA, emphasizing its unsuitability for inter-thread synchronization and MMIO, with alternatives like cuda::atomic_ref and inline PTX.

pages.title: Vulkan Interoperability
pages.summary: Details Vulkan-specific interoperability, including matching CUDA devices to Vulkan physical devices via UUIDs, importing memory objects via file descriptors, NT handles, named handles, and KMT handles, and mapping them as device pointers or mipmapped arrays.

pages.title: 10.20 Warp Match Functions
pages.summary: Warp match functions perform broadcast-and-compare operations of a variable between threads within a warp. Supported by compute capability 7.x+. Includes __match_any_sync and __match_all_sync. Requires mask parameter. Do not imply memory barriers.

pages.title: Warp Matrix Functions
pages.summary: Verbatim source-backed fallback page for Warp Matrix Functions.

pages.title: Warp Reduce Functions
pages.summary: Verbatim source-backed fallback page for Warp Reduce Functions.

pages.title: Warp Shuffle Functions
pages.summary: Verbatim source-backed fallback page for Warp Shuffle Functions.

pages.title: 10.19 Warp Vote Functions
pages.summary: Warp vote functions allow threads in a warp to perform reduction-and-broadcast operations on integer predicates. Includes __all_sync, __any_sync, __ballot_sync, and __activemask. Requires mask parameter specifying participating threads. Do not imply memory barriers.

pages.title: Dynamic Global Memory Allocation and Operations
pages.summary: Dynamic global memory allocation is supported on compute capability 2.x and higher. Functions include __device__ malloc(), __nv_aligned_device_malloc(), free(), memcpy(), and memset(). Memory is allocated from a fixed-size device heap (default 8 MB, configurable via cudaDeviceSetLimit). Allocated memory persists for the context lifetime and can be shared across threads and kernel launches. It cannot be freed via host-side runtime APIs, nor can host-allocated memory be freed via device free(). Examples cover per-thread, per-block, and cross-kernel-persistence allocation patterns.

pages.title: 10.18.3 __assume()
pages.summary: Compiler optimization hint. Allows compiler to assume Boolean argument is true. Undefined behavior if false at runtime. Unspecified behavior if argument has side effects.

pages.title: 10.18.2 __builtin_assume()
pages.summary: Compiler optimization hint. Allows compiler to assume Boolean argument is true. Undefined behavior if false at runtime. Unspecified behavior if argument has side effects.

pages.title: 10.18.1 __builtin_assume_aligned()
pages.summary: Compiler optimization hint. Allows compiler to assume pointer is aligned to at least align bytes. Returns argument pointer. Supports two-parameter and three-parameter versions (with offset).

pages.title: 10.18.4 __builtin_expect()
pages.summary: Compiler optimization hint. Indicates expected value of expression for branch prediction. Returns value of exp.

pages.title: 10.18.5 __builtin_unreachable()
pages.summary: Compiler optimization hint. Indicates control flow never reaches this point. Undefined behavior if reached at runtime.

pages.summary: Returns generic pointer obtained by executing PTX cvta.const instruction on rawbits value.

pages.summary: Returns generic pointer obtained by executing PTX cvta.global instruction on rawbits value.

pages.summary: Returns generic pointer obtained by executing PTX cvta.local instruction on rawbits value.
